topic: Error Handling - Custom Error Enums with Display
content: The single most common mistake beginners make when learning Rust error handling is assuming that a simple integer or string is enough to describe what went wrong, which leads to brittle code that fails immediately once the environment changes. You will see this in many tutorials where developers define a custom error type like `enum MyError { Io(i32), Parse(String) }` and then panic on every failure instead of gracefully propagating the specific cause back to the caller. This approach is fundamentally wrong because it discards context; when your application crashes or returns an error, you lose the ability to debug exactly which operation failed and why. The correct pattern involves building a multi-variant enum that captures both the type of error and the data associated with it, such as `enum NetworkError { ConnectionLost(String), Timeout(Duration) }`. To make these enums useful for production systems, you must implement the `Display` trait so users can see human-readable messages like 'Connection lost: timeout after 30 seconds' instead of cryptic internal states. You should also implement the `Debug` trait because debugging tools rely on it to generate stack traces and help developers inspect error values in a debugger window without needing to parse string representations manually. A frequent oversight here is forgetting to handle the lifetime constraints on generic types within the enum variants, which can cause compilation errors if you do not explicitly qualify your lifetimes like `fn process<T: Display>(input: &str) -> Result<Output<T>, MyError>` instead of omitting them entirely. When propagating these errors through functions using the `?` operator, you must ensure that every variant in your custom enum implements the `From` trait for all possible error types it might encounter; otherwise, the compiler will force you to match every specific error type manually, which is tedious and prone to missing edge cases. For instance, if you are parsing a configuration file from disk, you should implement `impl From<std::io::Error> for MyError` so that when you write `match config_file.read().map_err(MyError::Io)?`, the system automatically converts any underlying I/O error into your custom enum variant without writing explicit match arms. This is particularly important in systems programming where you want to avoid heap allocation, and Rust helps here by allowing you to implement no-alloc error types using enums that do not hold references or owned data unless necessary, keeping your application footprint small even under heavy load. If you are building a library that exposes multiple entry points with different failure modes, think of the `?` operator as a compiler-enforced contract: it guarantees that if your function returns an `Err`, it will only return one of the variants defined in your error enum, preventing accidental mixing of incompatible error types at runtime. Finally, remember that while you can use the standard library's `std::fmt` traits freely, custom implementations should follow a consistent naming convention for your variant fields so that logs remain readable across different parts of your codebase; this discipline prevents the chaos where one team uses 'timeout' and another uses 'timed out', making error aggregation impossible to automate later. By adhering to these patterns, you transform raw exceptions into actionable intelligence that helps developers fix issues faster rather than guessing what went wrong.

topic: Error Handling - Custom Error Enums with Display
content: Let's talk about constructing a custom error type, specifically using an enum to represent multiple distinct failure conditions in your application. Think of this as the Rust way to build a `Result<T, E>` where `E` is not just a simple integer or string, but a rich data structure capable of telling you exactly what went wrong and why. The mental model you need here is that an error enum is essentially a sum type from category theory; it represents a set of mutually exclusive possibilities. When we define `enum MyError { IoError(io::Error), ParseError(String), NotFound(ErrorCode) }`, we are creating three distinct variants, each capable of carrying its own payload. The first thing you'll notice is the syntax for these payloads; sometimes they take ownership like `IoError(io::Error)`, while other times they might take by value or reference depending on whether your specific variant needs to mutate that data later in the chain. Now comes the critical part: making this type usable in the rest of our ecosystem via traits from the standard library, specifically `Display` and `Debug`. These are implemented for us automatically when you derive them using `derive(Display, Debug)`, but there is a catch; if your enum contains variants that hold data which cannot be trivially serialized or formatted into strings—like an owned `String` in one variant or a complex struct in another—the macro expansion will fail to compile. This forces us to implement these traits manually by writing out the logic for each arm of our match statement. For instance, implementing `Display` requires you to manually format the inner data using `.to_string()` or string interpolation syntax like `$e.to_string().as_str()`. The same applies to `Debug`, though it is slightly less strict about formatting and often just calls into the internal methods of your struct to get a representation. But wait, there's another layer; what if we have nested errors? Say our `IoError` variant holds an actual `io::Error`, which itself wraps a system error code or message. To propagate this without copying data unnecessarily, we use the `From` trait from std::convert. The pattern is straightforward: implement `impl From<SomeNestedType> for MyError`. This tells Rust that whenever you try to convert `SomeNestedType` into `MyError`, it should automatically select the corresponding variant and copy over the inner value as ownership or reference. This allows us to chain operations using the `?` operator, which is where things get really interesting; when you write `return result?` in a function that returns our custom error type, Rust will automatically look for a `From` implementation from the immediate return type of the expression into our `MyError`. If it finds one, it converts and propagates; if not, compilation fails with a clear message telling us exactly which conversion is missing. This mechanism also supports no-alloc error types because we are often copying small integers or enum variants rather than allocating heap memory for every new instance of the error object. However, there is a debate about whether to use a union-like approach with `std::any::Any` or stick strictly to an explicit multi-variant enum; the answer depends on your needs. A plain enum like ours is generally preferred because it provides compile-time safety and clear documentation of all possible failure states at the type level, whereas `Any` requires runtime dispatch which can introduce performance overhead and runtime panic risks if you forget to handle a case. So when should you choose this approach? Choose multi-variant enums when your error domain is finite, well-defined, and you want to catch errors at compile time; avoid them only when your error space is truly infinite or dynamic enough that even an exhaustive match statement becomes impractical. One common pitfall people fall into is forgetting to implement `From` for specific nested types, leading to compilation errors that look like magic rather than logical gaps; always check the conversion path carefully. Another subtle issue arises with lifetimes and references; if you store a reference in one variant but an owned value in another, ensure your code handles both correctly or consider using interior mutability patterns like `RefCell` only when absolutely necessary to avoid borrow checker nightmares. Finally, remember that while `Display` is great for user-facing messages, `Debug` is often preferred by developers and debuggers because it shows the actual memory layout and variable names more explicitly, making troubleshooting significantly easier in a REPL or debugger session.
